strict_win_deltas.delta: P1's buildWorkingStock sorts each warehouse's lots once into a single reusable structure that drawFromWarehouse then iterates directly, while P2's sortedLotsForSku re-filters and re-sorts a warehouse's lots from scratch on every call (twice per single-warehouse line: once for the capacity check, again for the actual draw), duplicating the same sort logic across call sites instead of establishing one source of truth.

P2's parseSubmittedAt validates order.submitted_at against a strict ISO date/datetime regex (checking hour/minute/second ranges and calendar validity) before accepting it, whereas P1's submittedAtTimestamp falls back to the bare, implementation-defined Date.parse(value) for any non-numeric string, silently accepting non-ISO formats that shouldn't pass 'must parse as ISO dates or timestamps'.

P1 adds explicit regression tests for the single_warehouse 'reject despite sufficient combined stock' rule ('fulfill-wave rejects single-warehouse lines that require combined warehouse stock') and for the exact invalid-input stderr-object/exit-2 contract, while P2's test file only covers the two minimum-required accepted/rejected scenarios and leaves those spec-critical behaviors unverified by any test.

P1's parseInputFlag locates --input via argv.indexOf, tolerating any flag position or additional future flags, while P2's readInputPath hard-requires argv.length === 2 && argv[0] === '--input', so it throws immediately if any other argument is ever added or the flag isn't first.
